title: Fix Railway BEADS_API_KEY — sync truncated key with Netlify
description:
The Railway CLI's variables display truncates long values. When we read back BEADS_API_KEY after setting it, we only saw 40 chars of the 64-char key. We then set Netlify to that truncated value — now they're out of sync and Railway returns 401 to the Context Collector.

Fix (once Railway outage at status.railway.com clears):

  railway variables set BEADS_API_KEY=dbd7d6233d3cc24f15b9437882a13a7fdf41914a4a5d3bd3a28ae6d2b6b88093

That key is already set on Netlify and documented in .env.local.

Verify:
  curl -s -H 'Authorization: Bearer dbd7d6233d3cc24f15b9437882a13a7fdf41914a4a5d3bd3a28ae6d2b6b88093' \
    https://life-organizer-production-4c1f.up.railway.app/api/beads/ready

Then confirm the Context Collector returns non-empty beadsReady:
  curl -s https://life-organizer-mo.netlify.app/.netlify/functions/collect-world-state | jq .beadsError

Should be null (not '401').

## Scenarios

- Given the Railway outage has cleared
  When railway variables set BEADS_API_KEY=dbd7d6...8093 is run
  Then Railway redeploys and GET /api/beads/ready with that key returns issue data (not 401)

- Given the key is set correctly on Railway
  When the Context Collector Netlify function runs
  Then beadsError is null and beadsReady contains the current ready issues from Beads
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix server/index.js for Linux/Railway compatibility
description:
Three changes required before server/index.js can run in a Linux container on Railway.

Why this exists:
server/index.js was written for macOS/zsh. Railway runs Linux containers where zsh is not present and PORT is injected by the platform. These must be fixed before the Beads Service can be deployed.

Changes required:
1. PORT: change hardcoded `const PORT = 3001` to `const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3001`
   (Railway injects PORT; hardcoded 3001 causes bind failure)

2. Shell: change all `shell: '/bin/zsh'` in execSync calls to `shell: '/bin/bash'`
   (zsh not present in node:20-slim Linux images; bash is always available)

3. Verify: BDG_DIR uses `resolve(homedir(), 'beads-global')` which correctly resolves
   to /root/beads-global in the container — no change needed here.

Acceptance criteria:
- `npm run server` still works locally on macOS after changes
- server/index.js passes startup validation in a Linux Docker container
- /api/health returns ok=true on Railway after deploy

Part of: EPIC-INFRA (life-bsr)

## Scenarios

- Given server/index.js is deployed to Railway
  When Railway starts the container and injects PORT=8080
  Then the server binds to port 8080 and /api/health returns ok=true

- Given server/index.js is running in a node:20-slim Linux container
  When the startup validation runs
  Then bd is found via @beads/bd (npm), no zsh-not-found error occurs

- Given the changes are applied
  When `npm run server` is run locally on macOS
  Then the server starts on port 3001 (PORT env var not set locally) with no errors

- Given a request hits /api/beads/ready on the deployed Railway service
  When bd executes in /bin/bash
  Then issues are returned correctly (same output as local macOS)
close_reason: PORT now reads process.env.PORT || 3001; all shell: '/bin/zsh' changed to '/bin/bash'; error messages updated. Verified: server starts locally on macOS, /api/health returns ok=true.

title: Context Collector v1: Beads + Tasks adapters writing to World State
description:
First version of the Context Collector: reads from the deployed Beads Service and Supabase tasks table, normalises, and writes a unified world state snapshot. This is the first data actually flowing through the new architecture.

What it does:
On trigger (app open or manual refresh — polling only in v1, Dolt hooks deferred to life-43k):
1. Call deployed Beads Service GET /api/beads/ready → upsert into Supabase beads_ready table
2. Read Supabase open_tasks WHERE status != 'completed' → compute derived fields
3. Derived fields: tasks_overdue, tasks_due_today, tasks_due_this_week
4. Expose everything via useWorldState React hook

Implementation:
- New Netlify function: netlify/functions/collect-world-state.js
  • Fetches from Railway (BEADS_API_KEY auth), upserts beads_ready rows in Supabase (SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY)
  • Returns full world state snapshot to caller
- New React hook: src/useWorldState.js
  • Calls the Netlify function on mount and on manual refresh
  • Returns { beadsReady, openTasks, derived, loading, error, refresh }
- Wire useWorldState into LifeOrganizer.jsx replacing useBeadsTasks

Note: Calendar data NOT in v1 (life-5eu). Dolt hook trigger NOT in v1 (life-43k).
Service role key stays server-side in Netlify function only — never in client code.

Depends on: life-xqz (deployed Beads Service ✓), life-57m (World State schema ✓)
Part of: EPIC-INFRA (life-bsr)

## Scenarios

- Given the Railway Beads Service is reachable and has ready issues
  When the app opens or the user triggers a manual refresh
  Then the beads_ready table in Supabase is upserted with current ready issues and useWorldState returns them to the UI

- Given open_tasks rows exist in Supabase with deadlines
  When the Context Collector runs
  Then tasks_overdue, tasks_due_today, and tasks_due_this_week are computed correctly and exposed via the useWorldState hook

- Given the Railway Beads Service is unreachable
  When the Context Collector attempts to fetch Beads data
  Then the last-known beads_ready rows are preserved in Supabase, an error is logged, and the UI renders stale data with a staleness indicator rather than crashing

- Given no open tasks exist in Supabase yet
  When the Context Collector runs for the first time
  Then all derived counts are 0 and the UI renders an empty state without errors
close_reason: Context Collector deployed and working in production. Fixed PostgREST in-filter syntax ('completed','cancelled' → unquoted) and applied beads_ready schema migration (status + issue_type columns). World State sync confirmed working at life-organizer-mo.netlify.app.

title: Design and provision Supabase World State schema
description:
Create the Supabase project and schema that serves as the shared World State store for all agents. This is the central dependency for the entire intelligence layer.

Why:
Without World State, the Context Collector has nowhere to write, the Rules Engine has nothing to evaluate, and no agent can share context. This is the single most blocking infrastructure dependency after the Beads Service.

Account setup:
Sign up at supabase.com via GitHub OAuth. Create project in nearest region (us-east-1). Free tier: 2 active projects, 500 MB DB, 5 GB egress — sufficient indefinitely.
WARNING: Auto-pause after 7 days inactivity on free tier. Resume is instant but scheduled crons will fail during pause.

Irrevocable schema decisions (cannot easily change after data exists):
- ALL timestamps: timestamptz — never plain timestamp
- ALL JSON: jsonb — never json (supports indexing)
- Primary keys: bigint generated by default as identity — not UUID
- EVERY table must have user_id uuid column (for RLS, even single-user now)
- EVERY table must have created_at + updated_at with moddatetime() trigger
- Enable RLS on ALL tables from day one

Auth model: Supabase Auth + JWT. Server-side (Netlify Functions, Railway) uses SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY. PWA client uses SUPABASE_ANON_KEY + user JWT. Service role key NEVER in client code.

Tables to create:
  calendar_snapshot (id, user_id, event_date, events jsonb, created_at, updated_at)
  open_tasks (id, user_id, title, status, priority, time_required_minutes, deadline, source, source_url, created_at, updated_at)
  beads_ready (id, user_id, issue_id, title, priority, blocked_by jsonb, synced_at)
  rules (id, user_id, name, condition_type, condition_config jsonb, priority_threshold, cooldown_minutes, total_fires, accepted_count, dismissed_count, deprecated_at, created_at, updated_at)
  notification_log (id, user_id, rule_id, payload jsonb, fired_at, user_action, action_at, action_latency_seconds)
  recommendation_history (id, user_id, content, world_state_snapshot jsonb, user_action, created_at)
  rule_suggestions (id, user_id, suggestion_type, target_rule_id, original_value jsonb, suggested_value jsonb, reasoning, confidence, approved, approved_at, applied_at, created_at)
  user_preferences (id, user_id, notifications_enabled, quiet_hours_start, quiet_hours_end, email, timezone, auto_apply_threshold, updated_at)

Implementation steps:
1. Create Supabase project.
2. Run schema SQL in Supabase SQL editor (add moddatetime extension, create tables, add indexes on user_id and timestamp columns, enable RLS, add RLS policies).
3. Seed rules table with 4 v1 seed rules from ARD v2.
4. Add to 1Password: SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_ANON_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY.
5. Add to local .env.local: VITE_SUPABASE_URL, VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY.

## Scenarios

- Given an empty Supabase project (just created)
  When the migration SQL runs in the Supabase SQL editor
  Then all 8 tables are created, RLS is enabled on each, moddatetime triggers are installed, and a test INSERT + SELECT through the anon key respects the user_id filter

- Given the World State schema is provisioned
  When a Netlify Function using the service role key inserts a row into open_tasks
  Then the row is visible server-side, but a client-side request using the anon key only sees rows where user_id matches auth.uid()

- Given the Supabase project on the free tier hasn't been accessed for 7 days
  When any app request hits Supabase
  Then the project resumes within ~2s and the request completes — scheduled cron failures during the pause window are expected and acceptable

- Given a row in any table is updated
  When the UPDATE completes
  Then the moddatetime() trigger fires and updated_at reflects the current timestamp
close_reason: Supabase project provisioned at vmjcqxuxltzskuudfsri.supabase.co. All 8 tables created with RLS, moddatetime triggers, and indexes. 4 seed rules inserted. Credentials in .env.local, Netlify, and Railway.

title: Deploy Beads Service as persistent cloud endpoint
description:
Deploy server/index.js as a persistent cloud service so cloud functions and the Context Collector can read Beads data without local machine access.

Why:
The current server/index.js runs on localhost:3001 during development. Cloud functions (Netlify, scheduled jobs) have no way to reach it. Deploying it as a persistent service eliminates the local/cloud divide for Beads data.

Host: Railway (confirmed). Always-on Node.js, ~$5/mo, simplest deploy story.

Prerequisites (must be complete first):
- life-6zf: Dolt remote configured (DONE — mofro/beads-global on DoltHub)
- life-men: server/index.js Linux compatibility fixes (PORT, shell)

Implementation:
1. Write Dockerfile in repo root:
   - Base image: node:20-slim
   - Install dolt binary (curl from github.com/dolthub/dolt/releases)
   - Run: npm install -g @beads/bd  (installs Linux bd binary — official npm package)
   - Copy server/index.js and package.json
   - Write startup script: bd init (if needed), bd dolt remote add, bd dolt pull, node server/index.js

2. Connect Railway to GitHub repo (life-organizer), set root directory, set start command.

3. Set Railway environment variables:
   - DOLT_REMOTE_USER=mofro
   - DOLT_REMOTE_PASSWORD=<DoltHub token from 1Password>
   - BEADS_API_KEY=<generate — store in 1Password>
   - SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY (once Supabase is set up)

4. Add BEADS_SERVICE_URL + BEADS_API_KEY to Netlify env vars and local .env.local.

5. Verify: GET /api/health returns ok=true from Railway public URL.

Note: write operations (claim, close) flow through this service → Dolt remote → local bd dolt pull keeps local in sync.

Part of: EPIC-INFRA (life-bsr)

## Scenarios

- Given the Dockerfile and railway.toml are committed to the repo and Railway is connected to the GitHub repo
  When Railway triggers a deploy from main
  Then the container builds successfully, the startup script runs bd init → dolt pull → node server/index.js, and GET /api/health returns {ok: true}

- Given the Beads Service container is running on Railway
  When a local `bd dolt push` syncs new issue data to DoltHub
  Then a subsequent GET /api/beads/ready on the Railway URL returns the updated issues (sync runs on that endpoint)

- Given the container starts for the first time with no existing beads data in the image
  When the startup script runs
  Then bd init creates ~/beads-global, dolt pull populates it from DoltHub, and the server starts cleanly with valid data

- Given DoltHub is unreachable when the container starts
  When the dolt pull in the startup script fails
  Then the server starts anyway with empty or stale local data and logs a warning — it does not crash
close_reason: Deployed to Railway at https://life-organizer-production-4c1f.up.railway.app — /api/health returns ok=true, bd 1.0.4, BEADS_DIR=/app confirmed. Auth middleware live.

title: EPIC: Configure Dolt remote for beads-global
description:
Set up a Dolt remote so beads-global state can be accessed from the cloud. This unblocks deploying the Beads Service.

Why this first:
The deployed Beads Service (server/index.js) needs to sync from a remote Dolt repo on startup. Without a configured remote, it can only serve whatever state was baked into the container at build time — useless for live data.

Implementation:
1. Evaluate DoltHub (managed, free tier, simple setup) vs. self-hosted Dolt server.
2. Create remote: bd dolt remote add origin <url>
3. Initial push: bd dolt push origin main
4. Verify: clone the remote on a separate machine, confirm issues are visible.
5. Add bd dolt push to the standard session-close workflow (already in CLAUDE.md).
6. Document remote URL in .notes and README.

Recommendation: DoltHub unless there are privacy concerns about issue content being in a managed service.

title: EPIC: Infrastructure Foundation
description:
The foundational layer that collapses the local/cloud divide and provides the shared World State store. Everything in the system depends on this epic being complete first.

Components:
1. Dolt remote configuration for beads-global (DoltHub or self-hosted)
2. Deployed Beads Service (server/index.js as a persistent cloud service)
3. Supabase World State schema (calendar_snapshot, tasks, rules, recommendations, notification_log, user_preferences, patterns tables)
4. Context Collector v1 (Beads + Tasks adapters writing to World State on event trigger)

Why this first:
Without the Dolt remote, Beads data is invisible to cloud functions. Without the deployed Beads Service, no agent can read Beads issues. Without the World State schema, no agent has anywhere to write. Without Context Collector v1, the World State is empty. Every other epic reads from or writes to this foundation.

See ARD v2 §Implementation Sequence and §Beads-as-a-Service for full rationale.

title: Google OAuth infrastructure: server-side auth code flow for Calendar + Gmail
description:
Both Calendar (life-5eu) and Gmail (life-9br) require server-side OAuth with refresh tokens stored in Supabase. Building them on the same infrastructure is cheaper than two separate flows. This issue provisions that shared foundation.

Scope:
- Single Google Cloud project, single OAuth 2.0 client (Web App type)
- Consent screen scopes: calendar.readonly + gmail.readonly
- One Netlify Function: GET /api/auth/google/callback — exchanges auth code for access + refresh tokens
- One token refresh helper (called by Calendar adapter and Gmail cron before each API call)
- Refresh tokens stored in Supabase user_preferences (per ARD secrets inventory)

Setup steps:
1. Google Cloud Console: enable Calendar API + Gmail API on the same project
2. OAuth consent screen: add both scopes, mark Internal (personal use — no Google review)
3. Create OAuth 2.0 credentials (Web App): add redirect URIs localhost:5173/api/auth/google/callback + Netlify domain equivalent
4. Add GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID (Netlify + .env.local) and GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET (Netlify only)
5. Implement GET /api/auth/google/callback Netlify Function: exchange code, store refresh tokens in Supabase user_preferences
6. Implement POST /api/auth/google/refresh helper: takes grant type + stored refresh token, returns fresh access token
7. Add Connect Google button in PWA settings panel that initiates the auth code flow

Note: GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET is already in the ARD secrets inventory as a Netlify-only secret — this was anticipated from the start.

## Scenarios

- Given a logged-in user clicks 'Connect Google' in the PWA settings panel
  When the OAuth consent flow completes and Google redirects to /api/auth/google/callback with a valid auth code
  Then the callback function exchanges the code for access + refresh tokens, stores the refresh token in user_preferences.google_refresh_token in Supabase, and redirects the PWA to /settings?google=connected

- Given a refresh token stored in user_preferences for a user
  When the /api/auth/google/refresh Netlify function is POSTed with the user's session token
  Then it reads the stored refresh token, calls Google's token endpoint, and returns a fresh access token (valid for 1 hour) — without exposing the refresh token to the browser

- Given the Connect Google button has been clicked and the user completes consent
  When the callback receives an auth code that Google has already invalidated (e.g. code reuse or expired)
  Then the callback returns a 400 error JSON response and the PWA shows 'Google connection failed — please try again' messaging

- Given a user_preferences row that already has a google_refresh_token set
  When the user visits /settings
  Then the Connect Google button shows as 'Google Connected' (disabled/success state) rather than prompting re-auth
notes:
E2E verified 2026-05-22. Three bugs discovered and fixed post-initial-close:
1. GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET/ID not actually saved to Netlify (MCP call silently failed) — fixed by setting via CLI.
2. Netlify env:set --scope + --context flags required specific ordering; vars now set in 'all' context.
3. auth-google-callback used UPDATE which is a silent no-op when user_preferences row absent — changed to UPSERT. Seeded user_preferences row via REST API.
Final state: Settings shows 'Google Connected' badge on cold reload (server-side auth-google-status returns connected:true from Supabase). google_refresh_token column SET (103 chars) for user 37b3da7e. Commits: 5c0195a feat, e0f902b close, 8a621d7 SW fix, 69c0db3 upsert fix. All pushed to origin/main.
close_reason: Migration applied. Code committed + pushed. GCP OAuth client created (life-organizer-497121), Calendar + Gmail APIs enabled. Redirect URIs set for localhost:8888 + life-organizer-mo.netlify.app. Netlify env vars GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID, GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET, VITE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID all set. Test user g.mofro@gmail.com added to OAuth consent screen. Ready for end-to-end test.

title: Decide Calendar OAuth flow: client-side session vs. server-side refresh token
description:
life-5eu currently plans a client-side Google Identity Services (GIS) implicit token flow — access token stored in sessionStorage, expires in 1 hour, only available when PWA is open.

This is correct IF calendar data will always be user-session-only (Context Collector only runs when the user opens the app).

It is WRONG IF calendar data is needed for background work (e.g. the hourly Rules Engine cron sweep, or a server-side Context Collector trigger).

The ARD's secrets inventory lists 'Calendar OAuth refresh token → Supabase per-user row' — implying server-side flow was intended.

Options:
  A. Client-side only (GIS implicit): simpler, no Netlify Function needed for auth, token dies when tab closes. Rules Engine cron would have no calendar data outside user sessions.
  B. Server-side auth code flow: one-time consent in browser, refresh token stored in Supabase, calendar data available to all server functions. More setup, but consistent with how Gmail OAuth is designed.

Impact: decision affects life-5eu architecture, the hourly Rules Engine cron's access to calendar data, and whether the Context Collector can run server-side.
notes: CRITICAL CONTEXT MISSING FROM THIS ISSUE: The hourly Rules Engine cron (evaluate-rules.js) reads calendar_snapshot FROM SUPABASE — it never calls Google Calendar API directly. The cron does not need a Google token. The only component that needs a Google token is the Calendar adapter (life-5eu), which writes to calendar_snapshot. So the real question is: how fresh must calendar_snapshot be, and is user-session-only refresh acceptable? ARD event bus lists Context Collector as triggered by 'App opened' and 'manual refresh' — background calendar collection is NOT in the v1 design. IMPLICATION: Option A (client-side GIS implicit) is likely correct for v1. Server-side (Option B) is only needed if you want calendar_snapshot refreshed in the background while the PWA is closed. Decide: is daily staleness acceptable (user opens app once a day → snapshots are <24h old) or do you want fresher data?
close_reason: Decision: server-side auth code flow. life-8li now covers the full Google OAuth infrastructure (Calendar + Gmail) as a shared foundation. life-5eu depends on life-8li.

title: Context Reasoner: on-demand Claude recommendations from World State
description:
On-demand Claude call that takes full World State and returns rich ranked recommendations with explanations. Replaces the current rules-based scoring algorithm in LifeOrganizer.jsx.

This is the 'What should I work on right now and why?' button.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Where Claude Belongs):
Claude is given: calendar today (free blocks, committed time), open tasks (all sources, priority/deadline), Beads issues ready, recent user actions (what was accepted/dismissed). Returns: ranked list with specific reasons tied to current context, not generic priority scores.

Example output:
  1. life-b45 (Rules Engine v1) — your 2pm meeting just ended, you have 90 min free, this is a 1-2h task, and it unblocks 3 other issues
  2. Reply to Sarah's email — implicit deadline (she needs an answer today), 15 min
  3. life-57m (World State schema) — P0 blocker, nothing else can proceed without it

Implementation:
1. Create Netlify function: POST /api/recommend with worldState payload.
2. Call Anthropic API: structured prompt with world state sections, request ranked JSON response.
3. Cache response in Supabase recommendations table (invalidate on world state change).
4. Add 'Ask Claude' button to PWA recommendation panel.
5. Store user_action (accepted/dismissed) against each recommendation for Pattern Analyzer.

Depends on: life-b45 (Rules Engine v1 — need rule firing history for context)
Part of: EPIC-INTEL (life-39b)

title: Rules Engine v1: 4 seed rules evaluating World State
description:
Implement the deterministic Rules Engine that evaluates a seed set of rules against World State on every state change. No AI involved — pure condition evaluation.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Rules Engine Pattern):
Rules are stored in Supabase rules table. Engine reads enabled rules, evaluates each condition against current World State, checks cooldown via notification_log, fires matches to Notification Dispatcher.

4 seed rules to implement:
  open-block-match: free_block.duration >= task.time_required AND task.priority IN (high, critical) AND task.status = pending → 'You have {duration} free — {task} fits'
  deadline-proximity: task.deadline - now <= 24h AND task.status != completed → 'Deadline tomorrow: {task}'
  beads-unblocked: beads_issue.all_blocked_by_closed = true AND priority <= P1 → '{issue} is now unblocked'
  stalled-deadline: days_since_progress >= 3 AND deadline - now <= 7 days → '{task} has stalled — {N} days left'

Implementation:
1. Create evaluateRules(worldState, rules) pure function.
2. Check cooldowns against notification_log.
3. Write matches to notification_log (fired_at, rule_id, context_payload).
4. Expose rule match events for Notification Dispatcher to consume.
5. Run on: app open, manual refresh, and Supabase realtime subscription to tasks/calendar_snapshot tables.

Depends on: life-4qr (Context Collector v1 — needs World State data to evaluate)
Part of: EPIC-INTEL (life-39b)

## Scenarios

- Given open_tasks has a task with deadline within 24h and status != completed
  When the Rules Engine evaluates World State
  Then deadline_proximity fires, a row is written to notification_log with the rule_id and task context, and the match is returned to the caller

- Given a rule fired less than cooldown_minutes ago (recorded in notification_log)
  When the Rules Engine evaluates the same rule against the same World State
  Then the rule is skipped and no duplicate notification_log row is written

- Given open_tasks is empty and beads_ready has no unblocked P0/P1 issues
  When the Rules Engine runs
  Then no rules fire, notification_log is unchanged, and the engine returns an empty matches array without errors

- Given a Beads issue in beads_ready has all its blocked_by entries resolved (empty array) and priority <= P1
  When the Rules Engine evaluates World State
  Then the issue_unblocked rule fires and a notification_log row is written with the issue context

NOTES
Code deployed to Netlify (netlify/functions/evaluate-rules.js) with 4 seed rules: deadline_proximity, issue_unblocked, task_stalled, open_block. NOT yet tested — open_tasks table in Supabase is empty. Next session: (1) claim, (2) write scenarios, (3) seed test tasks in Supabase, (4) verify each rule fires correctly, (5) close with evidence.
notes: Code deployed to Netlify (netlify/functions/evaluate-rules.js) with 4 seed rules: deadline_proximity, issue_unblocked, task_stalled, open_block. NOT yet tested — open_tasks table in Supabase is empty. Next session: (1) claim, (2) write scenarios, (3) seed test tasks in Supabase, (4) verify each rule fires correctly, (5) close with evidence.
close_reason: Rules Engine verified in production. deadline_proximity fires for task with 12h deadline; cooldown correctly suppresses re-fire 5s later. notification_log insert confirmed working with channel/title/body columns. issue_unblocked/task_stalled/open_block no-op correctly with no matching data.

title: EPIC: Intelligence Layer
description:
The deterministic Rules Engine and the two AI-powered reasoning agents. This is where the system earns the word 'intelligent'.

Components:
1. Rules Engine v1: evaluate 4 seed rules against World State on every state change. Deterministic, no AI, near-zero cost.
2. Context Reasoner: on-demand Claude call with full World State → ranked recommendations with explanations. User-triggered.
3. Pattern Analyzer: daily/weekly Claude call reviewing rule firing history → suggest rule adjustments. One call per cycle.

Rule evaluation is fast, cheap, and always-on. Claude is invoked at exactly two points: when the user asks for advice (Context Reasoner), and when the system reviews its own behaviour (Pattern Analyzer).

Depends on: EPIC-INFRA (Rules Engine needs World State to evaluate against)

See ARD v2 §Rules Engine Pattern and §Where Claude Belongs.

title: EPIC: Context Collection
description:
All structured data sources that feed the World State. Each source is a pluggable adapter in the Context Collector. This epic adds Calendar and refines the Beads/Tasks adapters from EPIC-INFRA.

title: Calendar Context Collector — Google Calendar adapter feeding World State
description:
Personal Google Calendar is the first structured source to plug into the Context Collector layer. This makes calendar data available to the Rules Engine for free-block matching and schedule-aware recommendations.

Architectural context (ARD v2):
This is a Context Collector adapter, not a standalone feature. Its output goes into the World State (Supabase calendar_snapshot), where the Rules Engine can evaluate open-block-match and schedule-aware rules against it.

Implementation plan:
1. Google Cloud Console: create OAuth 2.0 credentials (Web App). Scopes: calendar.readonly. Authorised origins: localhost:5173 + production URL.
2. Add VITE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID to .env.local and Netlify env vars.
3. Load Google Identity Services in index.html.
4. Create useGoogleCalendar hook: initTokenClient, requestAccessToken on mount, store token in sessionStorage.
5. Fetch events: GET /calendar/v3/calendars/primary/events (timeMin=now, timeMax=+7d, singleEvents=true).
6. Normalise to WorldState calendar_snapshot shape: {id, title, start, end, duration_minutes, type, free_block_before, free_block_after}.
7. Write snapshot to Supabase calendar_snapshot table (upsert on event id).
8. Handle token expiry: re-request on 401.
9. Depends on: EPIC-INFRA (World State schema must exist before this can write to it).

## Scenarios

_Fill in before claiming._

- Given [describe initial state]
  When [describe trigger or action]
  Then [describe expected outcome]
design: 1. Google Cloud Console: create project, enable Calendar API, create OAuth 2.0 credentials (Web App type). Authorized JS origins: http://localhost:5173 and https://life-organizer-mo.netlify.app. Authorized redirect URIs: same. Copy client ID. 2. Add VITE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID to .env.local (gitignored) and to Netlify env vars. 3. Load Google Identity Services in index.html: <script src='https://accounts.google.com/gsi/client'>. 4. Create useGoogleCalendar hook: tokenClient = google.accounts.oauth2.initTokenClient({client_id, scope:'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.readonly', callback}). Normalise to {id, title, start, end, date, type} shape matching existing CalendarContent component. Replace MOCK_CALENDAR. 7. Handle token expiry: re-request on 401.

SCENARIOS (filled before claim):

title: Beads write-back: claim and close from UI via local API
description:
Beads tasks are currently read-only in the UI. The accordion shows 'bd close <id>' as a text hint but cannot execute it. Since the local Express server is already running when the app is used locally, claim and close should be possible directly from the UI for that workflow.

Implementation Plan:
1. Add POST /api/beads/claim/:id to server — runs: bd update <id> --status=in_progress in ~/beads-global via zsh. Returns {ok, error}.
2. Add POST /api/beads/close/:id to server — accepts JSON body {reason}, runs: bd close <id> --reason='<reason>'. Returns {ok, error}.
3. In BeadsTaskRow expanded panel: show Claim button when task status is open/pending; Close button always visible.
4. Clicking Close reveals an inline reason input (text field + confirm/cancel) before POSTing — no modal.
5. Both buttons show tooltip 'server offline' and are disabled when /api/health check fails.
6. On success: re-fetch beads list. On failure: show inline error below the button.
7. Conservative approach: wait for server confirmation before updating UI (no optimistic update).

Close triggers an inline reason input (text field + confirm/cancel) before POSTing. Both buttons disabled with tooltip 'server offline' when /api/health fails. On success: refresh beads list. title: Persist tasks to Supabase open_tasks
description:
## Why

Tasks are currently stored only in localStorage — per-device, per-browser, lost on cache clear.
The Supabase open_tasks table already exists (from the world state schema) but the UI never
reads or writes to it. This makes task data fragile and prevents future features like email
intake populating tasks.

## What

Wire addTask, updateStatus, deleteTask, and completeTask to open_tasks in Supabase via a new
tasks.js Netlify function. Use optimistic updates so the UI stays snappy. Migrate any existing
localStorage tasks on first load.

## Scenarios

- Given a user adds a task via the form
  When the task is submitted
  Then it appears immediately in the list (optimistic) and is persisted to open_tasks in Supabase

- Given tasks exist in Supabase
  When the app is loaded on a fresh browser (no localStorage)
  Then tasks are loaded from Supabase and displayed correctly

- Given localStorage has tasks from before this change
  When the app loads for the first time after the update
  Then localStorage tasks are migrated to Supabase and localStorage is cleared

- Given the user changes a task status or deletes a task
  When the action is taken
  Then the UI updates immediately (optimistic) and the change is written to Supabase in the background
close_reason: tasks.js CRUD function deployed, LifeOrganizer.jsx switched from localStorage to Supabase with optimistic updates + localStorage migration. Verified build passes. Category migration SQL written — needs one manual run in Supabase SQL editor.

title: Decide + implement per-rule notification channel config
description:
life-xnb (Email dispatch) requires email to be opted in per rule. life-qlr (Web Push) and life-4ov (in-app) need the same. The rules table currently has no column to store which channels a rule fires to.

Decision needed (choose one):
  Option A: Add notification_channels jsonb column to rules table, e.g. ['email','push','in_app']. Requires migration.
  Option B: Store channel config inside condition_config jsonb (already exists). No migration needed, but mixing rule conditions with dispatch config is semantically messy.

Recommendation: Option A — the distinction between 'when to fire' (condition_config) and 'where to route' (notification_channels) is meaningful and worth a separate column.

Migration (Option A):
ALTER TABLE public.rules
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS notification_channels jsonb NOT NULL DEFAULT '["in_app"]';

Also update seed_rules migration or re-seed existing rows.
notes: SEED DATA INCONSISTENCY: Rule 3 (issue_unblocked) already uses notify_channel inside condition_config: {priority_max:1, notify_channel:'in_app'}. This is exactly Option B data. The migration must also clean this up — strip notify_channel from condition_config and write it to notification_channels instead (if Option A is chosen). EVALUATE-RULES.JS MUST BE UPDATED: all four condition evaluators currently hardcode channel:'in_app' in their return objects. When this issue is implemented, evaluate-rules.js must be updated to read rule.notification_channels and set channel accordingly. This is not mentioned in the description. life-4ov DEPENDENCY MISSING: life-4ov (in-app notifications) also needs to know which rules route to in_app channel — add bd dep add life-4ov life-v66 after this is resolved.

title: Migration: add source column to calendar_snapshot
description:
life-36d (Corporate Bridge) writes corporate events to calendar_snapshot with source='corporate'. The current calendar_snapshot schema has no source column — only id, user_id, event_date, events (jsonb), created_at, updated_at.

Without this column, there is no way to distinguish personal Google Calendar events from corporate iCal events in the snapshot.

Migration required:
ALTER TABLE public.calendar_snapshot
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS source text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'google';

Values: 'google' (personal Google Calendar), 'corporate' (iCal subscription), 'manual' (future)

Add to supabase/migrations/ with next timestamp prefix.
notes: STRUCTURAL FLAW IN MIGRATION AS WRITTEN: calendar_snapshot has UNIQUE (user_id, event_date) — only one row per day. Adding source as a row-level column means a second INSERT for the same date (e.g. corporate iCal events on a day that already has Google events) violates the constraint. Two valid approaches: OPTION A (row-per-source): Change UNIQUE (user_id, event_date) to UNIQUE (user_id, event_date, source). Then update evalOpenBlock in evaluate-rules.js to merge all matching rows for today (currently uses .find() which returns only the first). OPTION B (per-event source tag): Keep one row per date. Add source field inside each event object in the events JSONB array: {id, title, start, end, source, ...}. Context Collector merges Google and corporate events into one row, each event tagged. Rules Engine unchanged. RECOMMENDATION: Option B — the Rules Engine already handles events as a flat array, no code changes needed there. Implement as: no row-level column migration needed; add source to event objects when writing.

title: Add POST /api/beads/create endpoint to Beads Service
description:
life-lqn (Conversation Intake Agent) routes new Beads issues through the Beads Service REST API, but server/index.js has no create endpoint. Current routes are: health, sync, ready, list, show/:id, claim/:id, close/:id, stats. This must be added before life-lqn can be implemented.

Implementation:
1. Add POST /api/beads/create to server/index.js
2. Accept body: { title, description, type, priority } (matches bd create flags)
3. Execute: bd create --title='...' --description='...' --type=... --priority=... --json
4. Return the created issue JSON
5. Auth: existing Bearer token middleware already covers it
notes: SHELL INJECTION RISK: server/index.js bd() function runs execSync with a shell string. All existing endpoints pass only IDs (URL params, easy to validate). This is the first endpoint accepting free-text user input (title, description). Interpolating these directly into a shell command breaks on single quotes and is a code injection vector. Before implementing, decide on escaping strategy: (1) shellescape/shell-quote the values before interpolation, (2) write title/description to a temp file and pass as --description=@file, or (3) use --stdin-json if bd create supports it. Verify: does bd create --json return a parseable JSON object (like bd list does), or does it return a human-readable confirmation? Check bd create --help before writing the handler.

title: bdg read-path gap: bd repo sync changes not pushed to DoltHub
description:
When bdg runs a read command (list, ready, show, stats, etc.), bd repo sync pulls project changes into beads-global's local Dolt DB. But because reads don't trigger the post-commit hook, those synced changes sit in beads-global's local Dolt and never reach DoltHub — so Railway and Supabase stay stale until a write happens to come along.

Reproduction:
1. Close an issue in a project repo via `cd project && bd close <id>`
2. Run `bdg ready` — bd repo sync pulls the close into beads-global
3. Check DoltHub / Supabase — issue still shows as open (no push fired)
4. Run any bdg write — NOW the push fires and Supabase updates

Root cause:
The post-commit hook is only triggered in the write path of bdg(). The read path calls bd repo sync but has no mechanism to detect whether that sync produced new Dolt commits.

Fix:
After bd repo sync in the read path, check whether beads-global has unpushed commits. If it does, fire the post-commit hook. Candidate check:

  before=$(cd ~/beads-global && bd dolt status --json 2>/dev/null)
  bd repo sync >/dev/null
  after=$(cd ~/beads-global && bd dolt status --json 2>/dev/null)
  if [[ "$before" != "$after" ]]; then
    ~/beads-global/.beads/hooks/post-commit &
  fi

Or simpler: always fire the hook after bd repo sync, relying on the debounce to prevent hammering. The debounce trailing-fallback already handles burst suppression safely.

Depends on: none — standalone improvement to bdg() in ~/.zshrc
notes: WRITE-PATH GAP (related to read-path gap): the project-level post-commit hook (.beads/hooks/post-commit) fires immediately after a local bd write and calls the Context Collector. But Railway reads from DoltHub, and the chain from project write to DoltHub is: project .beads/ → bd repo sync in ~/beads-global → bd dolt push → DoltHub → Railway bd dolt pull. The hook fires before bd repo sync and bd dolt push have run, so Railway syncs from a stale DoltHub. Result: PWA shows closed issues as still open until a manual bd repo sync + bd dolt push + Railway sync is run. Correct fix: the hook should first run bd repo sync + bd dolt push, THEN call the Context Collector. Or the hook should live at the beads-global level, firing AFTER bdg pushes to DoltHub. Workaround: after any project-level bd write, run: cd ~/beads-global && bd repo sync && bd dolt push — then the hook (or a manual trigger) will pick up correct state.

title: Dolt post-commit hook: trigger Context Collector on bd write
description:
Wire a Dolt post-commit hook so that any local `bd` write (close, claim, create) automatically triggers the Context Collector Netlify function, keeping World State in near-real-time sync with Beads data.

Why deferred from life-4qr:
The Context Collector function (life-4qr) will expose an HTTP endpoint callable by anything. The hook itself is 3 lines of shell. Deferred because: (a) the function must exist first, (b) the hook fires on every bd write with no debounce — a busy session would hammer the collector — and (c) it introduces Dolt hooks infrastructure not yet tested.

Implementation:
1. Add debounce logic: track last-fired timestamp, skip if < N seconds since last call (suggest 30s).
2. Write .beads/hooks/post-commit:
   #!/bin/bash
   # Trigger Context Collector when Beads data changes.
   # Debounce: skip if fired within last 30s.
   LAST=$(cat ~/.beads-hook-last 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
   NOW=$(date +%s)
   if [ $((NOW - LAST)) -lt 30 ]; then exit 0; fi
   echo $NOW > ~/.beads-hook-last
   curl -s -X POST https://life-organizer-mo.netlify.app/.netlify/functions/collect-world-state \
     -H "Authorization: Bearer $BEADS_API_KEY" > /dev/null &
3. Make hook executable: chmod +x .beads/hooks/post-commit
4. Test: run bd close on a test issue, confirm World State updated in Supabase within ~5s.
5. Document BEADS_API_KEY requirement in CLAUDE.md (must be set in shell env for hook to auth).

Depends on: life-4qr (Netlify function must exist and be deployed first)

## Scenarios

- Given BEADS_API_KEY is set in the shell environment and life-4qr's Netlify function is deployed
  When `bd close` is run on any issue
  Then the post-commit hook fires, the Context Collector runs, and beads_ready in Supabase is updated within 5 seconds

- Given the hook fired less than 30 seconds ago
  When another bd write occurs
  Then the hook skips the HTTP call (debounce) and exits silently

- Given the Netlify function is unreachable when the hook fires
  When the curl call fails
  Then the hook exits silently — the bd operation completes normally and is not blocked
notes: INCORRECT IMPLEMENTATION: hook fires immediately after a project bd write and calls the Context Collector. But Railway reads from DoltHub, and the project changes haven't reached DoltHub yet at that moment. Chain required before calling Netlify: (1) cd ~/beads-global && bd repo sync, (2) bd dolt push, (3) THEN curl to Context Collector. Without steps 1-2, the Railway sync always pulls stale data. LATENCY CONCERN: adding repo sync + dolt push to every bd write will slow the hook by several seconds. This is acceptable — the alternative is a hook that silently does nothing useful. CORRECT HOOK BODY: replace the current curl block with: cd ~/beads-global && bd repo sync > /dev/null 2>&1 && bd dolt push > /dev/null 2>&1 && curl -s -X POST COLLECTOR_URL ... VERIFICATION: close a real issue, watch Supabase beads_ready update within ~15s. That is the actual acceptance test.
close_reason: Fixed: hook now runs bd repo sync + bd dolt push in ~/beads-global before calling Context Collector. End-to-end verified.

title: Email dispatch: Rules Engine → Resend for high-priority alerts
description:
Email delivery channel in the Notification Dispatcher. When the Rules Engine fires a high-priority rule match, send an email alert via Resend.

Architecture:
Replaces the original life-7pf (standalone Netlify cron with hardcoded deadline check). Email dispatch is now triggered by the Rules Engine, not a separate scheduled function. Any rule can be configured to notify via email channel.

Implementation:
1. Create Resend account (resend.com — free tier 3000 emails/month).
2. Add RESEND_API_KEY to Netlify env vars.
3. Create Netlify function: POST /api/notify/email with {rule_match, user_email, context}.
4. Template: subject from rule match type, body includes task title + deadline + context + direct link to PWA.
5. Deduplication: check notification_log before sending (same rule + same task + same day = skip).
6. Respect user_preferences.quiet_hours (don't send between quiet_hours_start and quiet_hours_end).
7. Channel selection: email is opted-in per rule (configurable in Settings).

Depends on: life-b45 (Rules Engine v1), life-57m (World State schema — needs notification_log)
Part of: EPIC-NOTIFY (life-k6l)

## Scenarios

_Fill in before claiming._

- Given [describe initial state]
  When [describe trigger or action]
  Then [describe expected outcome]
notes: QUIET HOURS CONFIRMED: user_preferences has quiet_hours_start (time) and quiet_hours_end (time) — both present in schema. MISSING: rules table has no notification_channels column. The issue assumes email is 'opted-in per rule (configurable in Settings)' but there is nowhere to store per-rule channel preferences. Options: (a) add notification_channels jsonb column to rules table via migration, or (b) store channel prefs in condition_config jsonb. Resolve before implementing. A custom domain (e.g. a personal domain you own) is required for production delivery.

title: Web Push infrastructure: background notifications via service worker
description:
True background push notifications — fires even when the PWA is not open. Requires service worker + VAPID keys + push subscription stored server-side.

Architecture:
The Rules Engine fires a rule match → Notification Dispatcher routes to push channel → Netlify function sends Web Push message → service worker receives and displays notification.

This is the background complement to the in-app notifications in life-4ov.

Implementation:
1. Generate VAPID key pair (web-push library).
2. Add VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY and VAPID_PRIVATE_KEY to Netlify env vars.
3. Service worker: handle 'push' event → display notification with title + body + action buttons.
4. PWA: on permission grant, call pushManager.subscribe() with VAPID public key. Store subscription endpoint in Supabase user_preferences.
5. Netlify function: given a rule match, call web-push.sendNotification(subscription, payload).
6. Integrate with Notification Dispatcher: push channel is selected based on user preference and notification priority.

Note: requires HTTPS in production (already satisfied by Netlify). iOS Safari support requires iOS 16.4+ with PWA installed to home screen.

Depends on: life-b45 (Rules Engine v1), life-57m (World State schema — needs subscription storage)
Part of: EPIC-NOTIFY (life-k6l)

## Scenarios

_Fill in before claiming._

- Given [describe initial state]
  When [describe trigger or action]
  Then [describe expected outcome]
notes: PWA MANIFEST CONFIRMED: vite-plugin-pwa generates the manifest (no static public/manifest.json). display: 'standalone' is already set in vite.config.ts — iOS Push requirement met. VAPID KEY EXPOSURE: VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY must be accessible browser-side for pushManager.subscribe(). In Vite, env vars are only exposed to the browser with a VITE_ prefix. Add as VITE_VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY (Netlify env var) and reference as import.meta.env.VITE_VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY in the PWA. VAPID_PRIVATE_KEY stays server-side only (no VITE_ prefix). title: Conversation Intake Agent: Claude session → Beads issues and tasks
description:
Build the Conversation Intake Agent: extracts tasks, commitments, and decisions from conversational text and creates Beads issues or personal tasks.

Why:
This closes the loop between thinking and doing. Conversations (with Claude, solo journaling, notes) are where commitments are made — but they currently disappear. This agent makes capture explicit, structured, and automatic.

Trigger: Explicit user invocation only (v1). User pastes conversation text into PWA form. Session-end automatic capture deferred until confidence gating is calibrated.

Input schema:
{ text: string, source: 'claude-session'|'note'|'voice-transcript'|'email'|'form', context?: 'work'|'personal'|'mixed', sourceUrl?: string }

Claude API call:
Single structured extraction call. Conservative system prompt defining TASK / COMMITMENT / DECISION / QUESTION / EVENT types. Output: typed JSON array of Extraction objects each containing { type, content: {title, description, deadline, priority}, destination: 'beads'|'personal-task', beadsProject?, confidence: 0.0-1.0, sourceSpan }

Confidence gate:
- >= 0.80: auto-create without user confirmation
- 0.50-0.79: surface in 'needs review' UI panel for user approval
- < 0.50: skip (shown collapsed for transparency)

Output routing:
- destination='beads': POST to Beads Service REST API → bd create
- destination='personal-task': INSERT to Supabase open_tasks table
- Source context determines destination: work conversation → beads; personal → tasks; mixed → ask

Edge cases to handle:
- All questions → extract as QUESTION type, not auto-created
- Past deadlines → downgrade to review regardless of confidence
- 'Sarah will handle X' → extract with owner='Sarah', surface for confirmation
- Long conversations → truncate to ~8000 tokens, note truncation in response

- ConversationIntakeWidget: textarea + context selector + submit button
- ConversationIntakeResult: auto-create list + needs-review checklist + confirm button

title: Corporate Bridge: iCal subscription + email forwarding setup

Architecture (ARD v2 §Corporate Walled Garden):
Full Exchange/Outlook integration requires IT approval and corporate OAuth. The ceiling is iCal URL subscription + user-configured forwarding rules — ~70% of scheduling value at zero IT involvement.

3. Poll iCal URL on same schedule as Google Calendar.

5. Document forwarding rule setup in README.

title: Email Intake Agent: Gmail parsing → World State tasks
description:
Claude-powered agent that reads personal Gmail, classifies each email, extracts actionable items, and writes them to World State.

Architecture (ARD v2 §Intake Layer as a First-Class Architectural Concern):
Email is where a large fraction of real commitments live — often implicit, rarely formatted as tasks. Rules can't parse 'could you take a look before Friday?' — Claude identifies the implicit ask, estimates urgency from sender context, and creates a task with appropriate confidence score.

Classification categories:
  meeting invite → create calendar event + optional prep task
  explicit deadline → create task with extracted deadline
  implicit ask → create task flagged low-confidence, surface for user confirmation
  notification/FYI → log to World State context, no task
  noise → discard

Implementation:
1. Gmail OAuth: add gmail.readonly scope to existing Google OAuth flow.
2. Poll Gmail (on schedule or app-open) for unprocessed emails (last 24h).
3. For each email: Claude call with subject + sender + body → classification JSON.
4. High-confidence results: write to Supabase tasks directly.
5. Low-confidence results: surface in PWA as 'Did you mean to add this?' prompt.
6. Track processed emails (message ID) to avoid reprocessing.
7. User can adjust classification rules in Settings.

Trigger: scheduled (daily) + manual ('check email for tasks' button in PWA)

Depends on: life-b45 (Rules Engine v1 — output goes to World State which rules evaluate)

title: Pattern Analyzer: daily Claude call for rule refinement
description:
Build the Pattern Analyzer: a weekly Claude call that reviews rule firing history and suggests rule improvements.

Why:
Rules are the executable memory of learned behaviour. Without a feedback loop, rules never improve. The Pattern Analyzer closes that loop: one Claude call per week reviews what fired, how users responded, and suggests threshold/cooldown adjustments or new rules.

Trigger: Netlify scheduled function, weekly (Monday 2am UTC).
  netlify/functions/pattern-analyzer.mts with config: { schedule: '0 2 * * 1' }

Input (read from Supabase):
- rules table: active rules with total_fires, accepted_count, dismissed_count, deprecated_at
- notification_log: per-rule firing history with user_action, action_at, action_latency_seconds
- Computed: acceptance rate, dismissal rate, avg action latency, fires-by-hour pattern

Claude API call:
Single call per cycle. Conservative system prompt: only suggest changes with clear evidence (high-volume dismissal, temporal pattern, rule conflict). Returns 1-2 high-confidence suggestions max as JSON.

Suggestion types:
- threshold_adjustment: raise/lower priority_threshold
- cooldown_adjustment: change cooldown_minutes
- new_rule: formalise an observed pattern not yet captured
- deprecate: disable a rule with near-zero acceptance rate

Output stored in: Supabase rule_suggestions table
Fields: suggestion_type, target_rule_id, original_value jsonb, suggested_value jsonb, reasoning, confidence, approved (null=pending)

Human review vs auto-apply:
- Phase 4 launch: ALL suggestions require human approval (email link to approve/reject)
- Trust mode (later): confidence >= 0.85 auto-applies; rest surface for review
- Hard cap: max 2 auto-applied changes per cycle

Feedback loop prevention:
- Never suggest the same change twice within 30 days
- If rule changed in last 7 days, require >= 0.90 confidence before re-suggesting

Notification: email to user when suggestions are ready (via Resend, same as EPIC-NOTIFY)

Depends on: Rules Engine v1 firing history must exist (life-b45)

title: EPIC: Notification & Dispatch
description:
The Notification Dispatcher: routes Rules Engine output to the right channel (in-app, push, email) with appropriate priority and cooldown management.

Components:
1. In-app notifications (life-4ov revised): browser Notification API, Rules Engine-triggered, cooldown via notification_log
2. Web Push infrastructure: service worker registration, VAPID keys, push server (Netlify function or dedicated), background delivery
3. Email dispatch via Resend: Rules Engine → email for high-priority rule matches. Resend free tier (3000/month). Replaces standalone cron approach.

Design principle: the dispatcher is dumb routing. Intelligence (what to notify) lives in the Rules Engine. The dispatcher only decides how and whether to route (channel, deduplication, quiet hours).

Depends on: EPIC-INTEL (Rules Engine must exist to trigger dispatch)

See ARD v2 §Decision 10 and §Rules Engine Pattern.

title: EPIC: Intake Agents
description:
Agents that convert unstructured, ambiguous external signals into structured World State entries. This is the layer that captures obligations from email, corporate context, and conversations — the inputs that a simple task list would miss entirely.

Components:
1. Email Intake Agent: Gmail → classify (meeting invite / deadline / implicit ask / noise) → extract → World State. Claude-powered.
2. Corporate Bridge: iCal URL subscription for work calendar + optional email forwarding rules. Partially rule-based.
3. Conversation Intake Agent: Claude session → extract tasks/commitments/decisions → confidence-gate → Beads issues or Supabase tasks.

Key principle: source determines destination. Work brainstorming → work Beads issues. Personal planning → life tasks. No manual labelling.

Depends on: EPIC-INTEL (output flows through Rules Engine into World State)

See ARD v2 §Intake Layer as a First-Class Architectural Concern and §Conversation as Work Stream.

title: Beads close with inline reason prompt in accordion

4. Track acknowledgement in Supabase notification_log (prevents re-firing within cooldown).

title: Unified task list: manual tasks + Beads interleaved
description:
## Why

Manual tasks (open_tasks), Beads issues (beads_ready), and eventually email-intake tasks
all live in separate UI sections. The user sees three separate lists instead of one
prioritized inbox. The data is already normalised to a common shape — all three sources
produce objects with {id, title, status, priority, deadline, source, ...}.

## What

Replace the separate Tasks section and Beads section with a single unified task list,
sorted by: overdue first → deadline ascending → priority → source. Each row still shows
its source badge (manual / beads / email). The QuickStats counts should span all sources.

The separate BeadsTaskRow accordion (with description/notes/commands) can be preserved as
an expand-on-click panel inside the unified list.

## Scenarios

- Given manual tasks and Beads issues both exist
  When the app loads
  Then all tasks appear in a single list, sorted by urgency/priority

- Given a task is overdue and a Beads issue has no deadline
  When the list renders
  Then the overdue task appears above the Beads issue

- Given the user clicks a Beads task row
  When it expands
  Then the description, notes, and terminal commands panel is shown (existing BeadsTaskRow behaviour)

- Given no tasks or Beads issues exist
  When the list renders
  Then a single empty state message is shown

title: Schedule task as Google Calendar event
description:
## Why

Tasks have a timeRequired field but no way to block time for them. The Google Calendar
OAuth infrastructure (auth-google-refresh, the enabled Calendar API, stored refresh token)
is already in place. We should let users turn any task into a calendar event with one tap.

## What

Add a "Schedule →" button to TaskRow. Tapping it posts to a new schedule-task Netlify
function that fetches a fresh Google access token, creates a calendar event with the task
title and timeRequired duration, and stores the returned event ID back on the task
(source_url or a new calendar_event_id column).

The UI should show a calendar badge on tasks that have a linked event.

## Scenarios

- Given a task with timeRequired set
  When the user taps "Schedule →"
  Then a calendar event is created with the task title and timeRequired duration, starting
  at the next available hour

- Given a task without timeRequired
  When the user taps "Schedule →"
  Then a default 30-minute event is created

- Given a task already linked to a calendar event (has calendar_event_id)
  When the user views the task
  Then a calendar badge/link is shown instead of the "Schedule →" button

- Given the calendar sync runs after scheduling
  When the app checks for today's events
  Then the newly scheduled event appears in the Calendar section

title: EPIC: UI & Polish
description:
User-facing improvements that don't block the core system but meaningfully improve the experience. These can proceed largely independently of the infrastructure/intelligence epics.

Existing issues in this epic:

as good as the data and intelligence underneath it.

4. Month view: 5-row × 7-column grid. Each cell shows day number + up to 3 event dots (colored by type). Overflow shown as '+N more'.
5. Clicking a day cell in month view expands that day's events in an inline popover below the grid.
6. All views consume the same normalised {id, title, start, end, date, type} shape from useGoogleCalendar — no view-specific fetching.

  Then [describe expected outcome]

Work week: Mon-Fri of current week, grouped by day header, events as time-sorted list per day — no pixel-positioned grid needed. Month: 5-row × 7-col grid, each cell shows day number + up to 3 event dots (colored by type), overflow shown as '+N more'. Click a day cell in month view to expand that day's events in a popover. title: Netlify scheduled function for urgent task email notifications
description:
Server-side hourly check for tasks with deadline approaching, sends email digest. Requires Supabase (tasks must be server-accessible — localStorage is client-only). Prerequisite: Supabase integration must be complete.

Implementation Plan:
1. Install dependencies in netlify/functions/: npm install @supabase/supabase-js resend.
2. Create netlify/functions/notify-urgent.mts.
3. In netlify.toml add: [functions.notify-urgent] schedule = "0 * * * *" (runs hourly).
4. Function logic: init Supabase with service key (bypasses RLS). Query tasks WHERE deadline < NOW() + INTERVAL '24 hours' AND status = 'pending', joined to auth.users for email address.
5. Check notifications_sent table (task_id, sent_at) — skip tasks already notified. Send remaining via Resend API (resend.com — free tier 3000/month): subject 'Due tomorrow: <title>', plain text body with title, deadline, and bd close hint.
6. Insert sent records into notifications_sent to prevent duplicate emails on the next run.
7. Required Netlify env vars: RESEND_API_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY, SUPABASE_URL.
8. Depends on: life-bok (Supabase integration must be live first).
design: 1. npm install @supabase/supabase-js resend (in netlify/functions/). 2. Create netlify/functions/notify-urgent.mts. In netlify.toml add: [functions.notify-urgent]\nschedule = '0 * * * *'. 3. Function logic: init Supabase with service key (bypasses RLS). Query: SELECT tasks.*, auth.users.email FROM tasks JOIN auth.users ON tasks.user_id=auth.users.id WHERE deadline < NOW()+INTERVAL '24 hours' AND status='pending'. 4. For each result, send via Resend API (resend.com — free tier 3000/month, simple REST API): subject 'Due tomorrow: <title>', plain text body with title + deadline + bd close hint. 5. Track sent notifications in a notifications_sent table (task_id, sent_at) to avoid duplicate emails. 6. Env vars needed in Netlify dashboard: RESEND_API_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY, SUPABASE_URL.

title: Supabase integration for cross-device task storage
description:
Manual tasks live in localStorage only — no cross-device sync, no server-side access for scheduled notifications, and lost if browser data is cleared. Supabase provides PostgreSQL with Row Level Security, a JS client, and magic-link auth — unlocking cross-device sync and unblocking server-side email notifications.

Implementation Plan:
1. Create Supabase project at supabase.com. Run schema: CREATE TABLE tasks (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, user_id UUID REFERENCES auth.users, title TEXT, category TEXT, priority TEXT, time_required INT, deadline TIMESTAMPTZ, status TEXT, source TEXT DEFAULT 'manual', source_url TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(), updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()); ALTER TABLE tasks ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; CREATE POLICY 'own tasks' ON tasks USING (auth.uid()=user_id) WITH CHECK (auth.uid()=user_id).
2. npm install @supabase/supabase-js.
3. Add VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY to .env.local (gitignored) and to Netlify env vars.
4. Create useSupabaseTasks hook that mirrors the current useState+localStorage pattern but reads/writes to Supabase.

title: Design and generate real PWA icons
description:
Current icons are placeholder blue circles generated with Python. PWA install prompt and home screen show these — embarrassing for a tool you actually use daily. Need real branded icons before sharing or using the app seriously.

Implementation Plan:
1. Design the icon: 'LO' monogram or a calendar/organizer glyph on blue-600 (#2563eb) background. Must be readable at 48px and sharp at 512px. Create SVG as the canonical source file.
2. Export PNG assets: 192×192 → public/icon-192.png, 512×512 → public/icon-512.png, 180×180 → public/apple-touch-icon.png. Tools: Figma (free), Canva, or hand-coded SVG + Inkscape/sharp for PNG export.
3. Add apple-touch-icon to index.html <head>: <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">.
4. Add theme-color meta if not present: <meta name="theme-color" content="#2563eb">.
5. Verify vite-plugin-pwa config / public/manifest.json references the correct icon paths and sizes.
6. Test PWA install prompt on iOS Safari and Android Chrome — confirm home screen icon renders correctly at both sizes.

notes: MANIFEST LOCATION: vite-plugin-pwa generates the manifest dynamically from vite.config.ts — there is no static public/manifest.json. Icons are defined in the plugins([VitePWA({ manifest: { icons: [...] } })]) section of vite.config.ts. To update icon paths or add apple-touch-icon, edit vite.config.ts (not a JSON file). APPLE-TOUCH-ICON: still needs to be added to index.html <head> — vite-plugin-pwa does not inject it automatically. Add: <link rel='apple-touch-icon' href='/apple-touch-icon.png'>. THEME COLOR: already present in vite.config.ts manifest as '#2563eb'.

Process: end-to-end verification for multi-system beads. Any bead whose acceptance scenario crosses more than one system (local Dolt → DoltHub → Railway → Supabase → PWA, or local code → Netlify → external API) must be verified at the FINAL consumer, not at the point of implementation. 'Stamp written', 'curl fired', 'function returned 200', 'hook ran' are all intermediate signals — not acceptance. The real test is: does the end user experience (PWA, UI, downstream service) reflect the change? Close only after verifying the last step in the chain. Corollary: write acceptance scenarios that name the final consumer explicitly, not the implementation artifact.
